# Turbopuffer: architecture, implementation, and the object-storage vector database landscape

**Turbopuffer is a vector database that treats object storage (S3/GCS/Azure Blob) as its source of truth [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/) — not a cold tier — achieving 10–100× cost reduction over incumbents while serving 2.5 trillion+ documents in production.** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/) This architectural bet, enabled by S3's strong consistency (2020) and compare-and-swap (2023), [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) has attracted Cursor, Notion (10B+ vectors), Linear, [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) and Superhuman. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Jxnl +2")](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&) Written entirely in Rust, turbopuffer's stateless compute nodes inflate cached data from object storage like a pufferfish — cold queries at ~343ms, warm queries at **8ms p50** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) — making inactive namespaces cost nearly nothing. This document provides a complete technical deep-dive into turbopuffer's internals, a production-ready Rust implementation architecture following its approach, and a rigorous comparison with every major vector database that touches object storage.

---

## Part 1: Turbopuffer architecture deep dive

### The object-storage-first storage engine

Turbopuffer's founding insight is that three technological shifts invalidated the assumption that vector databases need in-memory storage: **NVMe SSDs** (4–5× slower than DRAM but 100× cheaper), [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) **S3 strong consistency** (December 2020), and **S3 conditional writes** (December 2023). [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "567-labs")](https://567-labs.github.io/systematically-improving-rag/talks/turbopuffer-engine/) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) The storage engine is an LSM-tree that persists directly to object storage, [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Terencezl")](https://terencezl.github.io/blog/2026/02/03/case-study-turbopuffer-ann-v3/) where any node can compact data, latency is high but throughput is phenomenal, individual writes are expensive but storage is cheap. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer)

The system runs as a cluster of `./tpuf` Rust binaries [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) in two roles: `./tpuf query` (stateless query nodes behind a load balancer) and `./tpuf indexer` (asynchronous index builders). Every namespace maps to a simple object storage prefix [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) — `s3://tpuf/{org_id}/{namespace}/` — containing `/wal` and `/index` directories. There are **no dependencies in the critical path other than object storage itself**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer) Much of the compute runs on spot instances, constantly exercising failover paths, [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer) contributing to **99.99% uptime since launch**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer)

The three-tier storage hierarchy drives turbopuffer's economics:

| Tier | Cost/GB/mo | Latency | Role |
|------|-----------|---------|------|
| Object storage (S3/GCS) | ~$0.02 | 200–500ms | Source of truth |
| NVMe SSD cache | ~$0.10 | Tens of ms | Warm cache |
| RAM | Higher | Sub-10ms | Hot cache |

This yields an effective cost of **~$70/TB/month** (S3 + SSD cache) versus **$3,600/TB/month** for traditional RAM + 3× replicated SSD architectures [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "567-labs")](https://567-labs.github.io/systematically-improving-rag/talks/turbopuffer-engine/) — a **51× difference**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer) One terabyte holds approximately 120 million documents with 1536-dimensional vectors and 2KB text data. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/turbopuffer)

### Write path: WAL on object storage

Every write appends a new file to the WAL directory inside the namespace's object storage prefix. When a write returns successfully, data is **guaranteed durably committed to object storage**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) The write path enforces a batching discipline: each namespace can commit **1 WAL entry per second**, and concurrent writes to the same namespace are batched into the same entry. If a new batch starts within one second of the previous one, it blocks up to 1 second before committing. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) Maximum payload size is **512 MB per request**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/write)

Write types include upserts, patches, deletes, and conditional writes. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/write) Conditional writes evaluate their predicate and apply the mutation atomically — the only operation in turbopuffer that achieves **Serializable isolation**. The `patch_by_filter` and `delete_by_filter` operations execute in two phases with re-evaluation guards to maintain consistency.

After WAL commit, data is **asynchronously indexed** by `./tpuf indexer` nodes for efficient ANN retrieval. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) Unindexed data (up to **128 MiB**) remains searchable via exhaustive brute-force scan of recent WAL entries, ensuring no data is invisible between write and indexing. Write latency is **p50 = 285ms** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/tradeoffs) for 500KB batches [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) — high relative to local-disk databases, but the tradeoff buys durability, simplicity, and statelessness. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/tradeoffs)

ACID properties are partial but intentional: **Atomicity** (all writes in an upsert batch apply simultaneously), **Consistency** (invariants maintained), **Durability** (object storage guarantees), but limited **Isolation** (no general read-write transactions).

### Indexing: SPFresh, RaBitQ, and the 100-billion-vector ANN v3

Turbopuffer's vector index is based on **SPFresh**, a centroid-based approximate nearest neighbor algorithm [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) that supports incremental updates. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/native-filtering) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) The choice of centroid-based over graph-based (HNSW/DiskANN) is driven by object storage access patterns: [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Medium")](https://dmitry-kan.medium.com/vector-podcast-simon-eskildsen-turbopuffer-69e456da8df3) a centroid index requires only **2 roundtrips for a cold query** (find nearest clusters, then fetch candidate vectors), while graph-based traversal requires a dozen or more sequential hops. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture)

SPFresh groups vectors into clusters represented by centroid vectors [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Substack")](https://whynowtech.substack.com/p/turbopuffer) (typically cluster means). At query time, the system compares the query to centroids first, then searches within the most promising clusters. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Substack +2")](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D) SPFresh dynamically splits and merges clusters as documents arrive or are deleted, maintaining balance without periodic full rebuilds. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/native-filtering) Turbopuffer extends this with **hierarchical nesting in a multi-dimensional tree structure** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) using a **100× branching factor** between levels — wide and shallow, matching the size ratio between DRAM and SSD (10–50×). [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3)

The **ANN v3 architecture** (January 2026) scales to 100 billion vectors at 1024 dimensions in f16 — **200 TiB of dense vector data** — serving over 1,000 QPS at **200ms p99 latency [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) with 92% recall@10**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) It achieves this through two techniques:

**Hierarchical clustering** maps naturally to the memory hierarchy. With a branching factor of 100, if all data vectors fit on SSD, all centroid vectors fit in DRAM. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) If all centroids fit in DRAM, upper-level centroids fit in L3 cache. Each tree level is ~100× smaller than the one below it, providing both spatial locality (nearby vectors stored contiguously) and temporal locality (upper levels accessed frequently, staying cached).

**Binary quantization via RaBitQ** compresses vectors by **16–32×** (f16 → 1 bit per dimension). RaBitQ exploits the concentration of measure phenomenon in high-dimensional spaces to provide tight theoretical error bounds on distance estimations. During search, the system evaluates quantized vectors first, uses error bounds to identify candidates possibly in the true top-k, then fetches full-precision vectors for reranking. **Less than 1% of data vectors need reranking**, dramatically reducing I/O. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3)

The memory hierarchy mapping for 100B vectors:

| Level | Data | Storage tier | Bandwidth |
|-------|------|-------------|-----------|
| Upper 3 tree levels (quantized centroids) | ~128 MiB | L3 CPU cache (504 MiB) | ~600 GB/s |
| Lowest tree level (quantized data vectors) | GBs | DRAM | ~300 GB/s |
| Full-precision vectors (~1% for reranking) | TBs | NVMe SSD | ~10 GB/s |
| All data (durable) | 200 TiB | Object storage | 1–10 GB/s |

CPU optimizations use **AVX-512 VPOPCNTDQ** instructions (512-bit popcount, 3-cycle latency, 1 instruction/cycle throughput) for binary distance computation, yielding a 30% kernel improvement and 5% end-to-end improvement. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) The system becomes **compute-bound at ~1,000 QPS per machine** due to the 64× higher arithmetic intensity from binary quantization — SSD and DRAM capacity remain underutilized.

For datasets exceeding a single machine's SSDs, turbopuffer uses **simple random sharding** across storage-optimized VMs [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3) (GCP z3, AWS I7i, Azure Lsv4 with 10–40 TiB NVMe). Queries broadcast to all shards, and global top-k is stitched from sub-results. Customers currently split into **4 TiB namespaces** pinned to individual SSDs. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3)

### Full-text search: custom BM25 on object storage

Turbopuffer built its own full-text search engine from scratch, optimized for the LSM-tree storage engine and object storage cold latency. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/fts) The **FTS v2** inverted index (January 2026) uses **fixed-size blocks of ~256 postings** (maintained via split >512 / merge <128 operations), independent of vector cluster boundaries. This replaced v1's cluster-aligned approach that produced tiny key-value pairs (median ~1.5 postings per partition) with poor compression. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/fts-v2-postings)

Compression uses Quickwit's bitpacking crate (Rust port of Daniel Lemire's simdcomp), operating on 128-posting frames at **741 million postings decoded per second** (~4.5 GB/s) — several times faster than Zstd. On 40M MSMARCO documents, FTS v2 reduced index size from 51.6 GiB to **5.22 GiB (9.9× reduction)**. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/fts-v2-postings)

The query algorithm uses **vectorized MAXSCORE over WAND**, optimized for long LLM-generated queries. MAXSCORE identifies "essential" versus "non-essential" terms and skips posting list blocks whose maximum possible score cannot affect the top-k [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/bm25-latency-musings) — providing rank-safe skipping with identical results to exhaustive evaluation. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/bm25-latency-musings) BM25 parameters (k1, b) are tunable, with language-aware stemming and stopword removal. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/fts)

### Native metadata filtering: neither pre- nor post-filter

Turbopuffer's attribute indexes are **aware of the primary vector index** and understand the clustering hierarchy. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/native-filtering) Documents are addressed as `{cluster_id}:{local_id}` in the vector index, and the attribute index uses these same addresses. The LSM key format is `(attribute_value, cluster_id)` with values being `Set<local_id>` compressed as bitmaps.

The query plan works in two steps: first find nearest clusters that contain at least one filter match (using cluster-level bitmap indexes), then evaluate matching candidates from those clusters only. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/native-filtering) This avoids both the recall problem of post-filtering (nearest vectors may not match filter) and the cost of pre-filtering (O(dimensions × matches)). The result is **>90% recall at ~25ms latency** with filters. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/blog/native-filtering)

Supported filter operators include Eq, NotEq, In, NotIn, Lt, Lte, Gt, Gte, Glob, [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) ContainsAny, ContainsAllTokens, ContainsTokenSequence, And, Or, Not. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) Attributes marked `filterable: false` receive a **50% storage discount** and improved indexing throughput. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/performance) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/write)

### Read path and caching behavior

Query routing sends client requests through a load balancer to query nodes, with subsequent queries routed to the same node for cache locality — though any query node can serve any namespace. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) The cold query flow requires **3–4 roundtrips** to object storage (each ~100ms): [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) download namespace metadata, navigate the first index level, then fetch cluster data via range requests. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) The warm query flow skips these roundtrips entirely.

The caching model follows the "pufferfish" metaphor — data inflates [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "567-labs")](https://567-labs.github.io/systematically-improving-rag/talks/turbopuffer-engine/) from object storage to disk to RAM as needed [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) and deflates when inactive. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) After a first query, namespace documents are cached on NVMe SSD. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) Cache refreshes on every query, ensuring latest writes appear on the next request. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) Clients can send **pre-flight warm cache requests** to hint that latency-sensitive queries are coming. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/architecture) [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "567-labs")](https://567-labs.github.io/systematically-improving-rag/talks/turbopuffer-engine/)

Query types supported span vector search (ANN and exact kNN), BM25 full-text search, hybrid search (multi-query combining vector + BM25 with client-side rank fusion), [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) attribute ordering, filter-only lookups, and aggregations (count, sum, min, max, distinct, group_by). [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) Multi-queries support up to **16 sub-queries per request** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) with snapshot isolation across them. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query)

### Consistency, multi-tenancy, and deployment model

**Strong consistency by default**: if you write, the next query sees the write. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer +3")](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D) The WAL on object storage guarantees durability. An optional **eventual consistency mode** drops warm latency from ~21ms to ~11ms p90 by skipping the object storage consistency check. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/tradeoffs) Over **99.99% of eventually-consistent queries return fully consistent data** because the same node typically serves reads and writes for a given namespace. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query) Stale data upper bound is 60 seconds; typical staleness is ~100ms. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/query)

Each namespace is an isolated document set at a distinct object storage prefix. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/write) The design guidance is to "make namespaces as small as they can be without routinely querying more than one at a time." [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/performance) Cursor uses one namespace per codebase across tens of millions of namespaces; [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/customers/cursor) Notion uses one namespace per workspace across millions. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) Inactive namespaces cost only object storage [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Jxnl")](https://jxnl.co/writing/2025/09/11/turbopuffer-object-storage-first-vector-database-architecture/) — approximately **$0.02/GB/month**.

Deployment options include multi-tenant shared clusters, single-tenant enterprise clusters, and **BYOC** (Bring Your Own Cloud) on AWS, GCP, or Azure with Kubernetes. The turbopuffer team operates via a secure control plane without direct VPC access. [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/byoc) The system is **SOC 2 Type 2 certified** [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/security) and **HIPAA compliant**, [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661& "Liveblocks")](https://liveblocks.io/blog/whats-the-best-vector-database-for-building-ai-products) with AES-256 encryption at rest [![](claude-citation:/icon.png?validation=AAD94E2A-98EB-4D8D-B761-4AFA8AFC9661&%3D%3D "Turbopuffer")](https://turbopuffer.com/docs/security) and optional per-namespace Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK).

---

## Part 2: Production-ready Rust implementation architecture

### High-level system design

The following architecture replicates turbopuffer's approach: object storage as source of truth, stateless compute nodes with tiered caching, centroid-based vector indexing optimized for minimal object storage roundtrips, and namespace-level multi-tenancy.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        Client SDKs                          │
│              (gRPC / REST / Python / TypeScript)             │
└───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                    ┌───────▼───────┐
                    │ Load Balancer  │
                    │ (sticky hash)  │
                    └───┬───┬───┬───┘
                        │   │   │
            ┌───────────┘   │   └───────────┐
            ▼               ▼               ▼
    ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐
    │  Query    │   │  Query    │   │  Query    │
    │  Node 0   │   │  Node 1   │   │  Node N   │
    │ (stateless│   │ (stateless│   │ (stateless│
    │  ./tpuf)  │   │  ./tpuf)  │   │  ./tpuf)  │
    └─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘
          │               │               │
          │  ┌────────────┼────────────┐  │
          │  │    NVMe SSD Cache       │  │  (Tier 2: ~$0.10/GB)
          │  │  ┌──────────────────┐   │  │
          │  │  │   RAM Cache      │   │  │  (Tier 1: hot data)
          │  │  └──────────────────┘   │  │
          │  └────────────┼────────────┘  │
          └───────────────┼───────────────┘
                          │
              ┌───────────▼───────────┐
              │   Object Storage      │    (Tier 3: ~$0.02/GB)
              │   (S3 / GCS / R2)     │
              │                       │
              │  /{org}/{ns}/wal/     │
              │  /{org}/{ns}/index/   │
              └───────────┬───────────┘
                          │
              ┌───────────▼───────────┐
              │   Indexer Nodes        │
              │   (async, stateless)   │
              │   ./tpuf indexer       │
              └───────────────────────┘
```

### Write path data flow

```
Client Upsert Request
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────┐
│ API Layer    │────▶│ Write Batcher│────▶│ WAL Writer          │
│ (validate,   │     │ (collect     │     │ (serialize batch,   │
│  parse,      │     │  concurrent  │     │  PUT to object      │
│  route)      │     │  writes for  │     │  storage as new     │
│              │     │  ≤1s window) │     │  WAL file)          │
└──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┬──────────┘
                                                     │
                                          ┌──────────▼──────────┐
                                          │ Indexing Queue       │
                                          │ (object storage)     │
                                          └──────────┬──────────┘
                                                     │
                                          ┌──────────▼──────────┐
                                          │ Indexer Node         │
                                          │ (read WAL entries,   │
                                          │  update centroid     │
                                          │  index, write index  │
                                          │  files back to S3)   │
                                          └─────────────────────┘
```

### Read path data flow

```
Client Query Request
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│ API Layer    │────▶│ Query Planner     │
│ (parse,      │     │ (choose strategy: │
│  validate)   │     │  ANN vs exact,    │
│              │     │  filter plan)     │
└──────────────┘     └────────┬─────────┘
                              │
                 ┌────────────▼────────────┐
                 │ Cache Lookup            │
                 │ (RAM → SSD → S3)       │
                 │                         │
                 │ HIT: return cached data │
                 │ MISS: fetch from S3,    │
                 │       populate cache    │
                 └────────────┬────────────┘
                              │
                 ┌────────────▼────────────┐
                 │ Search Execution        │
                 │ 1. Compare query to     │
                 │    cached centroids     │
                 │ 2. Identify top clusters│
                 │ 3. Fetch cluster data   │
                 │    (cache or S3 range)  │
                 │ 4. Score candidates     │
                 │ 5. Rerank with full-    │
                 │    precision vectors    │
                 │ 6. Apply metadata       │
                 │    filters              │
                 │ 7. Merge unindexed WAL  │
                 │    results              │
                 └────────────┬────────────┘
                              │
                 ┌────────────▼────────────┐
                 │ Return top-k results    │
                 └─────────────────────────┘
```

### Crate and module structure

```
vectordb/
├── Cargo.toml                    # workspace root
├── proto/
│   └── vectordb.proto            # gRPC service definitions
│
├── crates/
│   ├── vdb-api/                  # API server (gRPC + REST)
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── grpc.rs           # tonic gRPC service impl
│   │   │   ├── rest.rs           # axum REST handlers
│   │   │   ├── middleware.rs     # auth, rate limiting, tracing
│   │   │   └── routes.rs         # route definitions
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-storage/              # Object storage abstraction
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── backend.rs        # ObjectStore trait
│   │   │   ├── s3.rs             # S3/R2/MinIO backend
│   │   │   ├── gcs.rs            # GCS backend
│   │   │   ├── local.rs          # Local filesystem (dev/test)
│   │   │   ├── wal.rs            # WAL writer/reader
│   │   │   └── compaction.rs     # LSM compaction engine
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-cache/                # Tiered caching layer
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── memory.rs         # In-memory LFU cache (moka)
│   │   │   ├── ssd.rs            # NVMe SSD cache (direct I/O)
│   │   │   ├── tiered.rs         # Unified tiered cache manager
│   │   │   └── warming.rs        # Cache pre-warming logic
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-index/                # Vector + scalar indexing
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── centroid.rs       # SPFresh-style centroid index
│   │   │   ├── quantization.rs   # Binary quantization (RaBitQ)
│   │   │   ├── ivf.rs            # IVF-PQ fallback
│   │   │   ├── hnsw.rs           # HNSW (optional, for comparison)
│   │   │   ├── bm25.rs           # BM25 inverted index
│   │   │   ├── attribute.rs      # Attribute/metadata indexes
│   │   │   ├── distance.rs       # SIMD distance functions
│   │   │   └── builder.rs        # Async index builder
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-query/                # Query engine
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── planner.rs        # Query planning + optimization
│   │   │   ├── executor.rs       # Query execution engine
│   │   │   ├── filter.rs         # Metadata filter evaluation
│   │   │   ├── scorer.rs         # Vector scoring + reranking
│   │   │   ├── aggregation.rs    # count, sum, min, max, group_by
│   │   │   └── hybrid.rs         # Hybrid search (vector + BM25)
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-namespace/            # Namespace/tenant management
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── manager.rs        # Namespace lifecycle
│   │   │   ├── metadata.rs       # Namespace metadata on S3
│   │   │   └── schema.rs         # Document schema management
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   ├── vdb-types/                # Shared types and traits
│   │   ├── src/
│   │   │   ├── lib.rs
│   │   │   ├── vector.rs         # Vector types (f32, f16, binary)
│   │   │   ├── document.rs       # Document struct
│   │   │   ├── filter.rs         # Filter expression AST
│   │   │   ├── config.rs         # Configuration structs
│   │   │   └── error.rs          # Error types (thiserror)
│   │   └── Cargo.toml
│   │
│   └── vdb-observability/        # Metrics, tracing, logging
│       ├── src/
│       │   ├── lib.rs
│       │   ├── metrics.rs        # Prometheus/OTel metrics
│       │   ├── tracing.rs        # Distributed tracing setup
│       │   └── health.rs         # Health check endpoints
│       └── Cargo.toml
│
├── src/
│   └── main.rs                   # Binary entry point (query/indexer modes)
│
└── tests/
    ├── integration/              # Integration tests (testcontainers + MinIO)
    └── benchmarks/               # criterion benchmarks
```

### Core trait definitions

```rust
// === vdb-types/src/vector.rs ===

/// Supported vector element types
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum VectorData {
    F32(Vec<f32>),
    F16(Vec<half::f16>),
    Binary(Vec<u8>),  // 1 bit per dimension, packed
}

/// Distance metrics
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum DistanceMetric {
    Cosine,
    EuclideanSquared,
    DotProduct,
}

/// A document with vector embedding + metadata attributes
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct Document {
    pub id: DocumentId,
    pub vector: Option<VectorData>,
    pub attributes: HashMap<String, AttributeValue>,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize, Eq, PartialEq, Hash)]
pub enum DocumentId {
    U64(u64),
    Uuid(uuid::Uuid),
    String(String),  // max 64 bytes
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum AttributeValue {
    String(String),
    U64(u64),
    F64(f64),
    Bool(bool),
    StringArray(Vec<String>),
    Null,
}
```

```rust
// === vdb-storage/src/backend.rs ===

/// Abstract object storage backend
#[async_trait]
pub trait ObjectStore: Send + Sync + 'static {
    /// Read an object by key, returns bytes
    async fn get(&self, key: &str) -> Result<Bytes, StorageError>;

    /// Read a byte range from an object
    async fn get_range(
        &self, key: &str, range: Range<u64>,
    ) -> Result<Bytes, StorageError>;

    /// Write an object (atomic PUT)
    async fn put(&self, key: &str, data: Bytes) -> Result<(), StorageError>;

    /// Conditional write (compare-and-swap via ETag/generation)
    async fn put_if_match(
        &self, key: &str, data: Bytes, etag: Option<&str>,
    ) -> Result<PutResult, StorageError>;

    /// Delete an object
    async fn delete(&self, key: &str) -> Result<(), StorageError>;

    /// List objects under a prefix
    async fn list(&self, prefix: &str) -> Result<Vec<ObjectMeta>, StorageError>;

    /// Check if an object exists and return metadata
    async fn head(&self, key: &str) -> Result<ObjectMeta, StorageError>;

    /// Batch GET for multiple keys (scatter-gather I/O)
    async fn get_many(&self, keys: &[&str]) -> Result<Vec<Bytes>, StorageError> {
        let futs = keys.iter().map(|k| self.get(k));
        futures::future::try_join_all(futs).await
    }
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct ObjectMeta {
    pub key: String,
    pub size: u64,
    pub etag: String,
    pub last_modified: chrono::DateTime<chrono::Utc>,
}
```

```rust
// === vdb-storage/src/wal.rs ===

/// WAL entry representing a batch of writes
#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct WalEntry {
    pub sequence_number: u64,
    pub timestamp: u64,
    pub operations: Vec<WriteOperation>,
    pub checksum: u32,  // CRC32C
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum WriteOperation {
    Upsert(Vec<Document>),
    Delete(Vec<DocumentId>),
    DeleteByFilter(FilterExpression),
    Patch { filter: FilterExpression, updates: HashMap<String, AttributeValue> },
}

/// WAL writer that persists entries to object storage
pub struct WalWriter {
    store: Arc<dyn ObjectStore>,
    namespace: NamespacePath,
    batcher: WriteBatcher,
}

impl WalWriter {
    /// Enqueue a write. Batches concurrent writes into a single
    /// WAL entry (max 1 entry/second per namespace).
    pub async fn write(&self, ops: Vec<WriteOperation>) -> Result<u64, StorageError> {
        let entry = self.batcher.add(ops).await?;
        let key = format!("{}/wal/{:020}", self.namespace, entry.sequence_number);
        let data = bincode::serialize(&entry)?;
        self.store.put(&key, Bytes::from(data)).await?;
        Ok(entry.sequence_number)
    }
}
```

```rust
// === vdb-index/src/centroid.rs ===

/// SPFresh-style hierarchical centroid index
pub struct CentroidIndex {
    /// Tree levels, from root (level 0) to leaves
    levels: Vec<TreeLevel>,
    /// Branching factor (~100, matching DRAM/SSD ratio)
    branching_factor: usize,
    /// Distance metric
    metric: DistanceMetric,
    /// Configuration
    config: CentroidConfig,
}

pub struct TreeLevel {
    /// Centroid vectors for this level (quantized form in higher levels)
    centroids_quantized: Vec<BinaryVector>,   // 1 bit per dim
    /// Full-precision centroids for reranking
    centroids_full: Option<Vec<Vec<f32>>>,
    /// Mapping from centroid_id -> child range or posting list location
    children: Vec<ChildRef>,
}

pub enum ChildRef {
    /// Points to a range of centroids in the next level
    Internal { start: u32, count: u32 },
    /// Points to a posting list in object storage
    Leaf { object_key: String, offset: u64, length: u64 },
}

#[async_trait]
pub trait VectorIndex: Send + Sync {
    /// Search for top-k nearest neighbors
    async fn search(
        &self,
        query: &[f32],
        k: usize,
        filter: Option<&FilterExpression>,
        cache: &TieredCache,
        store: &dyn ObjectStore,
    ) -> Result<Vec<SearchResult>, IndexError>;

    /// Insert vectors into the index (called by indexer)
    async fn insert(
        &mut self,
        documents: &[Document],
    ) -> Result<(), IndexError>;

    /// Merge and compact index segments
    async fn compact(&mut self) -> Result<CompactionStats, IndexError>;
}
```

```rust
// === vdb-cache/src/tiered.rs ===

/// Three-tier cache: RAM -> NVMe SSD -> Object Storage
pub struct TieredCache {
    /// L1: In-memory cache (moka, W-TinyLFU eviction)
    memory: moka::future::Cache<CacheKey, Bytes>,
    /// L2: NVMe SSD cache (direct I/O, LRU eviction)
    ssd: SsdCache,
    /// L3: Object storage (source of truth)
    store: Arc<dyn ObjectStore>,
    /// Metrics
    metrics: CacheMetrics,
}

impl TieredCache {
    /// Read-through cache: RAM -> SSD -> Object Storage
    pub async fn get(&self, key: &CacheKey) -> Result<Bytes, StorageError> {
        // Check RAM cache
        if let Some(data) = self.memory.get(key).await {
            self.metrics.ram_hits.increment(1);
            return Ok(data);
        }

        // Check SSD cache
        if let Some(data) = self.ssd.get(key).await? {
            self.metrics.ssd_hits.increment(1);
            // Promote to RAM
            self.memory.insert(key.clone(), data.clone()).await;
            return Ok(data);
        }

        // Fetch from object storage
        self.metrics.s3_fetches.increment(1);
        let data = self.store.get(&key.to_object_key()).await?;
        // Populate SSD and RAM caches
        self.ssd.put(key, &data).await?;
        self.memory.insert(key.clone(), data.clone()).await;
        Ok(data)
    }

    /// Pre-warm a namespace into cache
    pub async fn warm(&self, namespace: &str) -> Result<(), StorageError> {
        let keys = self.store.list(&format!("{}/index/", namespace)).await?;
        let futs = keys.iter().map(|meta| self.get(
            &CacheKey::from_object_key(&meta.key)
        ));
        futures::future::try_join_all(futs).await?;
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

```rust
// === vdb-types/src/error.rs ===

use thiserror::Error;

#[derive(Error, Debug)]
pub enum StorageError {
    #[error("Object not found: {key}")]
    NotFound { key: String },
    #[error("Conditional write conflict (ETag mismatch)")]
    ConflictError,
    #[error("Object storage error: {0}")]
    BackendError(#[from] anyhow::Error),
    #[error("Serialization error: {0}")]
    SerializationError(#[from] bincode::Error),
    #[error("I/O error: {0}")]
    IoError(#[from] std::io::Error),
    #[error("Request timeout after {duration:?}")]
    Timeout { duration: std::time::Duration },
}

#[derive(Error, Debug)]
pub enum IndexError {
    #[error("Index corrupted: {reason}")]
    Corrupted { reason: String },
    #[error("Dimension mismatch: expected {expected}, got {actual}")]
    DimensionMismatch { expected: usize, actual: usize },
    #[error("Storage error: {0}")]
    Storage(#[from] StorageError),
}

#[derive(Error, Debug)]
pub enum QueryError {
    #[error("Namespace not found: {namespace}")]
    NamespaceNotFound { namespace: String },
    #[error("Invalid filter expression: {reason}")]
    InvalidFilter { reason: String },
    #[error("Index error: {0}")]
    Index(#[from] IndexError),
    #[error("Timeout: query exceeded {limit:?}")]
    Timeout { limit: std::time::Duration },
}
```

### Key dependency manifest

```toml
# Workspace Cargo.toml [dependencies] recommendations

# Async runtime
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }

# Object storage (choose one)
opendal = { version = "0.54", features = [
    "services-s3", "services-gcs", "services-fs",
    "layers-retry", "layers-logging", "layers-metrics"
] }
# OR for AWS-only:
# aws-sdk-s3 = "1.121"
# aws-config = { version = "1", features = ["behavior-version-latest"] }

# API layer
tonic = { version = "0.12", features = ["tls", "gzip"] }
prost = "0.13"
axum = { version = "0.7", features = ["json", "macros"] }
tower = { version = "0.4", features = ["timeout", "limit"] }

# Serialization
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
bincode = "1"
rkyv = { version = "0.8", features = ["validation"] }  # zero-copy for mmap'd vectors

# Caching
moka = { version = "0.12", features = ["future"] }      # W-TinyLFU in-memory cache
memmap2 = "0.9"                                          # Memory-mapped files for SSD

# Error handling
thiserror = "2"     # Library errors (matchable)
anyhow = "1"        # Application errors (contextual)

# Observability
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter", "json"] }
tracing-opentelemetry = "0.28"
opentelemetry = { version = "0.27", features = ["metrics"] }
opentelemetry-otlp = "0.27"

# Vector math / SIMD
half = { version = "2", features = ["num-traits"] }      # f16 support
wide = "0.7"                                              # Portable SIMD
simdeez = "1"                                             # x86 SIMD abstraction

# Utilities
bytes = "1"
uuid = { version = "1", features = ["v4", "serde"] }
parking_lot = "0.12"
crossbeam = "0.8"
chrono = { version = "0.4", features = ["serde"] }
config = "0.14"
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }

[build-dependencies]
tonic-build = "0.12"

[dev-dependencies]
criterion = { version = "0.5", features = ["async_tokio"] }
tempfile = "3"
proptest = "1"
testcontainers = "0.23"   # MinIO for S3 integration tests
```

### Configuration management pattern

```rust
// === vdb-types/src/config.rs ===

use serde::Deserialize;

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct ServerConfig {
    pub node_role: NodeRole,           // query | indexer
    pub listen_addr: String,           // "0.0.0.0:6333"
    pub grpc_port: u16,                // 6334
    pub storage: StorageConfig,
    pub cache: CacheConfig,
    pub index: IndexConfig,
    pub observability: ObservabilityConfig,
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub enum NodeRole { Query, Indexer }

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct StorageConfig {
    pub backend: StorageBackend,       // s3 | gcs | local
    pub bucket: String,
    pub region: String,
    pub endpoint: Option<String>,      // For MinIO/R2
    pub max_concurrent_requests: usize,// Default: 256
    pub request_timeout_ms: u64,       // Default: 30000
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct CacheConfig {
    pub memory_capacity_mb: u64,       // Default: 4096
    pub ssd_path: String,              // "/mnt/nvme/cache"
    pub ssd_capacity_gb: u64,          // Default: 500
    pub ttl_seconds: u64,              // Default: 3600
    pub warm_on_first_query: bool,     // Default: true
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
pub struct IndexConfig {
    pub branching_factor: usize,       // Default: 100
    pub target_recall: f32,            // Default: 0.95
    pub enable_quantization: bool,     // Default: true
    pub max_unindexed_bytes: u64,      // Default: 128 * 1024 * 1024
    pub compaction_interval_secs: u64, // Default: 300
}

// Load config: file -> env overrides -> CLI overrides
pub fn load_config() -> Result<ServerConfig, config::ConfigError> {
    config::Config::builder()
        .add_source(config::File::with_name("config"))
        .add_source(config::Environment::with_prefix("VDB").separator("__"))
        .build()?
        .try_deserialize()
}
```

### Observability setup

The observability stack uses `tracing` for structured logging and distributed traces, `opentelemetry` for metrics export, and health check endpoints for orchestrator probes. Every object storage call, cache lookup, index search, and WAL write is instrumented with spans carrying structured fields (namespace, latency, cache_hit, result_count). Separate Tokio runtimes for search versus write paths prevent write amplification from blocking read latency — a pattern used by Qdrant in production. The `#[instrument]` attribute macro on key functions provides automatic span creation with negligible overhead.

### Key design decisions and rationale

**IVF/centroid over HNSW for the index.** HNSW requires many sequential random-access hops — each a 100ms roundtrip on cold object storage. A centroid-based index (SPFresh/IVF) consolidates work into 1–2 bulk fetches of cluster data, reducing cold query latency from 10+ roundtrips to 3–4. The branching factor of 100 aligns with the DRAM-to-SSD capacity ratio, ensuring each tree level fits in the next-faster storage tier.

**Binary quantization (RaBitQ) over product quantization.** RaBitQ achieves 16–32× compression with tight error bounds, and the reranking step touches <1% of vectors. Binary distance computations map to CPU popcount instructions (AVX-512 VPOPCNTDQ), making the system compute-bound rather than I/O-bound — a better bottleneck because CPU scales linearly with money while SSD latency has hard physical limits.

**WAL on object storage rather than local disk.** This ensures durability without any local state, enabling truly stateless compute nodes that can be spot instances. The tradeoff is higher write latency (~285ms) but the batching discipline (1 entry/second, concurrent writes merged) amortizes the cost.

**Moka over custom LRU for RAM cache.** Moka implements W-TinyLFU admission policy (combining LFU frequency estimation with LRU recency), achieving near-optimal hit ratios for mixed access patterns. Its weight-based eviction handles variable-size vector blocks naturally, and the async API integrates cleanly with Tokio.

**OpenDAL over raw aws-sdk-s3.** OpenDAL provides a unified `Operator` abstraction across S3, GCS, Azure Blob, R2, MinIO, and local filesystem with composable middleware layers (retry, logging, caching, metrics, timeout). This eliminates per-backend code and makes testing trivial via the in-memory `Memory` backend.

---

## Part 3: Comparison with alternative vector databases on object storage

### The object storage adoption spectrum

Vector databases fall along a spectrum of object storage integration. At one end, **turbopuffer** and **Amazon S3 Vectors** treat object storage as the native substrate. In the middle, **Milvus** uses object storage as primary persistence but still requires compute nodes to load data into memory. At the other end, **Qdrant** and **Weaviate** use object storage only for snapshots or cold tenant offloading — not for serving queries.

**LanceDB** occupies a unique position: its Lance columnar format works natively on object storage (S3/GCS/Azure), but the open-source embedded version lacks the tiered SSD caching and automatic indexing that turbopuffer provides. The Enterprise version adds these features with a stateless query fleet and NVMe hybrid cache.

### Detailed comparison

| Dimension | turbopuffer | LanceDB (OSS/Enterprise) | Milvus 2.6 | Qdrant | Weaviate | S3 Vectors |
|-----------|------------|-------------------------|------------|--------|----------|------------|
| **Object storage role** | Source of truth | First-class backend | Primary persistence | Snapshots only | Backup + cold offload | IS the storage |
| **Open source** | No | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Yes (BSD-3) | No |
| **Language** | Rust | Rust | Go + C++ | Rust | Go | AWS internal |
| **Index algorithm** | SPFresh (centroid) + RaBitQ | IVF-PQ, IVF-HNSW | HNSW, IVF, DiskANN, GPU | Custom HNSW | HNSW, Flat, Dynamic | Undisclosed |
| **Cold query latency** | ~343ms (1M docs) | Hundreds of ms on S3 | Variable (segment load) | N/A | N/A (offloaded = unavailable) | ~100ms (frequent) |
| **Warm query p50** | **8ms** | Single-digit ms (Enterprise) | Milliseconds (in-memory) | <5ms | <10ms | ~100ms |
| **Write latency** | ~285ms (S3 WAL) | Low (local), high (S3) | Low (Kafka/Pulsar WAL) | Low (local WAL) | Low (local WAL) | Moderate |
| **Max proven scale** | **2.5T docs, 100B vectors queried** | Hundreds of millions | Billions | Hundreds of millions | Billions (with sharding) | 2B per index |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Namespace-per-tenant (100M+) | Table-per-tenant | DB/collection/partition | Collection-per-tenant | Class-per-tenant (hot/warm/cold) | Index-per-tenant |
| **Effective storage cost** | **~$70/TB/mo** | ~$20/TB (S3 only, no cache) | ~$20/TB S3 + compute | ~$600/TB (3× SSD) | ~$600/TB (SSD) | Very low |
| **Serverless** | Yes | Yes (Lambda + S3, Cloud) | Partial (Zilliz Cloud) | No | Partial (Cloud) | Yes |
| **FTS / BM25** | Yes (custom) | Yes (Tantivy) | Yes | Yes (sparse vectors) | Yes (BM25) | No |
| **Transactions** | Conditional writes only | ACID/MVCC | No | No | No | No |
| **Deployment complexity** | Zero (managed) | Low (embedded) to moderate | High (etcd + MQ + MinIO + nodes) | Low-moderate | Moderate | Zero (AWS) |

### When to choose each database

**Choose turbopuffer** when you need a fully managed, cost-effective vector search service with massive multi-tenant scale (millions of namespaces), can tolerate ~285ms write latency, and want zero operational overhead. Turbopuffer excels for RAG applications (Cursor, Notion) where most tenants are inactive and cold-start latency of ~400ms is acceptable for infrequent queries.

**Choose LanceDB** when you need open-source control, embedded operation (in-process, no server), multimodal data storage (images, video alongside vectors), ACID transactions with time-travel, or a data lakehouse approach where vectors are part of a larger analytical pipeline. LanceDB Enterprise competes directly with turbopuffer on managed features.

**Choose Milvus** when you need the broadest index algorithm support (GPU-accelerated CAGRA, DiskANN, SCANN), real-time streaming updates via Kafka/Pulsar, and you have the operational capacity to manage a distributed system. Milvus 2.6's Woodpecker WAL (writing directly to S3 at 750 MB/s) narrows the architectural gap with turbopuffer significantly.

**Choose Qdrant** when you need consistently low-latency queries (<5ms) without cold-start penalties, rich payload filtering with complex JSON conditions, and prefer a simpler Rust-based system at small-to-medium scale. Not suitable for cost-sensitive workloads with terabytes of infrequently-accessed data.

**Choose Amazon S3 Vectors** when you are AWS-native, need zero operational overhead, have moderate QPS requirements, and want deep integration with Bedrock Knowledge Bases and OpenSearch. Currently lacks full-text search and hybrid search capabilities.

---

## Part 4: Numbers, limits, and benchmarks

### Turbopuffer query latency (768 dimensions, 1M docs, ~3GB, top_k=10)

| Percentile | Warm (cached) | Cold (from S3) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| p50 | **8ms** | **343ms** |
| p90 | **10ms** | **444ms** |
| p99 | **35ms** | **554ms** |

With eventual consistency mode, warm queries drop to **sub-10ms** by skipping the S3 metadata check. The consistency floor is ~10ms for strong reads.

### ANN v3 at 100-billion-vector scale

The production cluster serving 100B vectors at 1024 dimensions (f16) achieves **200ms p99 latency** at **1,000 QPS** with **92% recall@10** — confirmed as global recall compared to true global top-k. This uses hierarchical clustering (branching factor 100) with RaBitQ binary quantization across storage-optimized machines (Intel Xeon 6980P class with 504 MiB L3 cache, 10–40 TiB NVMe). Each query scans ~500 data vector clusters (each containing ~100 vectors) per shard. Without quantization, the same workload would be bottlenecked at ~100 QPS (disk I/O bound).

### Production limits

| Metric | Observed in production | Hard limit |
|--------|----------------------|------------|
| Documents (global) | **2.5T+ at 8PB+** | Unlimited |
| Documents queried simultaneously | **100B+ at 10TB** | Unlimited |
| Documents per namespace | **500M+ at 2TB** | **500M at 2TB** |
| Namespaces | **100M+** | Unlimited |
| Max dimensions | — | **10,752** |
| Write batch size | — | **512 MB** |
| Write batch rate per namespace | — | **1 batch/s** |
| Concurrent queries per namespace | — | **16** |
| Multi-query sub-queries | — | **16** |
| Max document size | — | **64 MiB** |
| Max attribute value size | — | **8 MiB** |
| Max filterable value size | — | **4 KiB** |
| Max attribute names per namespace | — | **256** |
| Max ID size | — | **64 bytes** |
| Global write throughput | **10M+ writes/s at 32GB/s** | Unlimited |
| Per-namespace write throughput | **32k+ writes/s at 64MB/s** | 10k writes/s at 32 MB/s |
| Vector search recall@10 | **90–100%** | Continuously measured on 1% of live traffic |

### Cost comparison (independent Liveblocks test: 1536-dim, 1M reads, 1M writes, 10 namespaces)

| Provider | Monthly cost | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Turbopuffer** | **<$10** ($64 minimum) | Serverless, usage-based |
| Pinecone | $41 ($50 minimum) | Serverless |
| Qdrant Cloud | $27–$102 | Depends on quantization + disk caching |
| Chroma Cloud | $81 | Usage-based |

Customer-reported savings: **Cursor** saw costs drop **95%** (20× savings) after migration. **Notion** saved "millions of dollars" and removed all per-user AI charges while scaling to 10B+ vectors. Turbopuffer's published pricing: approximately **$1/month per million stored vectors** and **$4 per million queries**.

### Storage efficiency

Binary quantization via RaBitQ compresses vectors by **16–32×** versus f16 (1 bit per dimension versus 16 bits). At 1024 dimensions, a quantized vector is 128 bytes versus 2,048 bytes in f16 or 4,096 bytes in f32. Less than 1% of vectors require full-precision reranking. The FTS v2 inverted index achieves **9.9× size reduction** over v1 (5.22 GiB versus 51.6 GiB on 40M MSMARCO documents) through fixed-size 256-posting blocks with bitpacking compression at 741M postings decoded per second.

### How turbopuffer's cost model connects to architecture

The $70/TB/month effective cost comes directly from the architecture: **data at rest lives on object storage at $0.02/GB**, with only active query data promoted to NVMe SSD cache at $0.10/GB. Because compute nodes are stateless and run on spot instances, there is no replicated hot standby cost. Inactive namespaces consume zero compute resources — their entire cost is S3 storage. This is why Cursor (millions of codebases, most inactive) and Notion (millions of workspaces, subset active) see 10–100× savings: the traditional approach charges RAM prices for all data regardless of access frequency.

---

## Conclusion

Turbopuffer represents a genuine architectural shift in vector database design — not incremental improvement but a fundamentally different cost structure arising from treating object storage as a first-class citizen rather than a backup tier. The SPFresh centroid index minimizes object storage roundtrips to 2–4 per cold query. RaBitQ binary quantization shifts the bottleneck from I/O to compute (a more tractable problem). The LSM-tree WAL on S3 eliminates local state entirely, enabling spot-instance deployments.

The Rust implementation architecture described here replicates these key decisions: centroid-based indexing over HNSW (2 bulk fetches versus 12+ sequential hops), three-tier read-through caching with moka (W-TinyLFU) and direct NVMe I/O, WAL-per-namespace on object storage via OpenDAL, and namespace-level isolation as simple S3 prefixes. The critical Rust crate choices — OpenDAL for storage abstraction, moka for caching, tonic for gRPC, tracing-opentelemetry for observability — are battle-tested in production systems including Qdrant, InfluxDB IOx, and LanceDB.

The competitive landscape is converging: Milvus 2.6's Woodpecker WAL writes directly to S3, LanceDB Enterprise adds stateless query fleets with NVMe caching, and Amazon validated the thesis entirely by launching S3 Vectors in December 2025. The open question is not whether object-storage-first vector databases will dominate — that trajectory is clear — but whether managed services like turbopuffer or open-source alternatives like LanceDB/Milvus will capture the majority of production workloads. For teams prioritizing operational simplicity and multi-tenant economics, turbopuffer's current lead in production scale (2.5T documents, 100M+ namespaces) is substantial. For teams requiring open-source control, embedded operation, or custom index algorithms, LanceDB and Milvus offer compelling alternatives at the cost of additional operational complexity.
